intro:
Understanding how many calories your body needs is the foundation of any nutrition or weight management plan. Your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE) is the total number of calories you burn each day, combining your Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) — calories burned at rest — with energy from physical activity.

Our calorie calculator uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, the most scientifically validated formula for estimating BMR in modern adults. It accounts for your weight, height, age, gender, and activity level to give you personalized daily calorie targets for maintenance, weight loss, or weight gain.

With this information, you can determine exactly how many calories to eat to lose weight (create a deficit), maintain your current weight, or build muscle (slight surplus). Most nutrition experts recommend a 500-calorie daily deficit for approximately 1 pound of fat loss per week.
howItWorks: Enter your weight in kilograms, height in centimeters, age in years, gender, and activity level. The calculator computes your BMR using the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, then multiplies by your activity factor to get TDEE. It also shows calorie targets for weight loss (TDEE minus 500) and weight gain (TDEE plus 500).
interpretationGuide: Your BMR is the calories your body needs just to survive at rest — breathing, circulation, cell repair. Your TDEE includes all movement. To lose weight, aim to eat 300–500 calories below your TDEE. To gain muscle, eat 200–300 calories above TDEE with sufficient protein. Eating at TDEE maintains your current weight.

faqs.question: What is the Mifflin-St Jeor equation?
faqs.answer: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation calculates BMR as: 10 × weight(kg) + 6.25 × height(cm) − 5 × age + 5 for men, and 10 × weight(kg) + 6.25 × height(cm) − 5 × age − 161 for women. It was validated in studies as more accurate than older formulas like Harris-Benedict.

faqs.question: What activity level should I choose?
faqs.answer: Sedentary (desk job, little exercise), Lightly Active (light exercise 1–3 days/week), Moderately Active (moderate exercise 3–5 days/week), Active (hard exercise 6–7 days/week), and Very Active (physical labor or twice-daily training). When in doubt, choose the lower level — most people overestimate their activity.

faqs.question: How many calories should I eat to lose weight?
faqs.answer: A deficit of 500 calories per day creates approximately 1 pound (0.45 kg) of fat loss per week. For faster loss, a 750–1000 calorie deficit can yield 1.5–2 pounds per week, but going below 1200 calories (women) or 1500 calories (men) risks nutrient deficiencies and muscle loss.

faqs.question: How accurate are TDEE calculators?
faqs.answer: Calorie calculators are estimates with a margin of ±200–300 calories for most people. Individual variations in metabolism, genetics, and non-exercise movement (NEAT) mean you may need to adjust your intake based on real-world results after 2–3 weeks.

faqs.question: What is the difference between BMR and TDEE?
faqs.answer: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the calories your body burns at complete rest — doing nothing but keeping organs functioning. TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure) is your BMR multiplied by an activity factor to account for all movement throughout the day.

faqs.question: Do I need to count calories to lose weight?
faqs.answer: Calorie counting is one effective approach, but not the only one. Many people succeed with portion control, mindful eating, or food quality approaches. However, knowing your TDEE gives you a data-driven baseline to work from.

faqs.question: How many calories should I eat to gain muscle?
faqs.answer: A modest surplus of 200–300 calories above TDEE is generally recommended for lean muscle gain. Larger surpluses lead to more fat gain alongside muscle. Pairing a surplus with adequate protein (1.6–2.2g per kg body weight) and resistance training is key.

faqs.question: Why does my calorie need decrease with age?
faqs.answer: BMR decreases roughly 1–2% per decade after age 20, primarily due to loss of muscle mass. Since muscle is metabolically active tissue, maintaining it through strength training can slow this decline.

faqs.question: Should I eat back calories burned during exercise?
faqs.answer: This depends on which activity factor you selected. If you chose a level that already accounts for your exercise, do not eat back workout calories. If you selected 'sedentary' and then exercise, you may eat back 50–75% of estimated burn.

faqs.question: What is NEAT and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis (NEAT) is the energy burned through all movement that is not intentional exercise — walking, fidgeting, standing, doing chores. NEAT can vary by 1,000+ calories per day between individuals and explains why two people with the same TDEE estimate can have very different results.
